title: The 3-3-5 Revolution: Why {{ coach.DC }}'s Scheme Changes Everything
author: GatorVault Film Desk
excerpt: {{ coach.DC }}'s 3-3-5 isn't a gimmick — it's a personnel-driven answer to modern spread offenses. Here's how it works, who benefits, and why Jayden Woods makes it dangerous.
body:
When {{ coach.HC }} hired {{ coach.DC }} from Kentucky, the assumption was UF would run a traditional 4-2-5. Spring camp told a different story. {{ coach.DC }} installed a 3-3-5 hybrid that uses three down linemen to eat double teams, freeing five linebackers and hybrid defenders to flow to the ball.

The front is built around a dominant nose (Brendan Bett) and two versatile ends (LJ McCray, Jeremiah McCloud) who can kick inside on passing downs. The JACK position — occupied by Jayden Woods — is the scheme's centerpiece. Woods sets the edge against the run, drops into coverage on 3rd-and-medium, and rushes the passer on obvious passing downs. He's the reason this defense can stay in base personnel against 11 personnel spread sets.

In the secondary, the STAR/nickel spot is anchored by Kanye Clark, with veteran DJ Coleman rotating in as the second nickel (per post-spring depth charts from GatorCountry and Prep Prospect Report). {{ coach.DC }} wants a defender who can cover slot receivers and fit the run alley. The 3-3-5 lives and dies on assignment discipline — one missed fit creates a 40-yard explosive.

Spring film showed the install ahead of schedule. UF held its own against a talented offense in the spring game, and Woods recorded two TFLs and a strip-sack. The concern: depth behind the starting eleven. If injuries hit the linebacker room, {{ coach.DC }} may need to sub in more traditional 4-man fronts.
takeaways: 3-3-5 uses 3 DL + 3 LB + 5 DB — built for speed and versatility
Jayden Woods at JACK is the scheme's most important piece
STAR: Kanye Clark starts; DJ Coleman is the veteran nickel rotation
Depth is the biggest risk if injuries pile up mid-season

title: {{ coach.HC }}'s First 6 Months: A Real Assessment
author: GatorVault Staff
excerpt: Six months in, {{ coach.HC }} has rebuilt the roster through the portal, installed a new defensive identity, and kept the locker room bought in. We grade every major decision.
body:
{{ coach.HC }} inherited a program in transition. {{ former.Napier }}'s final season left questions at quarterback, offensive line, and defensive scheme. {{ coach.HC }}'s first move was hiring coordinators who fit his culture-first philosophy: {{ coach.DC }} on defense, {{ coach.OC }} on offense, and a revamped S&C staff under {{ staff.SC }}.

The 2026 portal class was the headline. UF added Eric Singleton Jr. (WR), Aaron Philo (QB), Lacota Dippre (TE), Cam Dooley (S), Evan Pryor (RB), and five offensive linemen among 23 incoming transfers. On paper, it addressed every weakness from 2025. Cormani McClain enrolled via portal last cycle and returns as the starting LCB — he is not part of this offseason's incoming class. The concern: five new OL starters need time to gel, and the QB battle between Tramell Jones Jr. and Philo remains unresolved.

Spring practice was encouraging. The defense looked ahead of schedule — {{ coach.DC }}'s 3-3-5 install clicked faster than expected. The offense showed flashes but struggled with consistency in the red zone. {{ coach.HC }}'s public comments have been measured; he hasn't named a QB starter and has emphasized "earning it in fall camp."

Recruiting is holding steady. The 2027 class sits at #5 nationally with Maxwell Hiller as the headliner. {{ coach.HC }}'s staff has maintained relationships from the {{ former.Napier }} era while adding their own pipeline in Georgia and Texas.

Our Year 1 projection: 8-4 with a bowl game. The floor is 6-6 if the OL struggles and the QB battle lingers into October. The ceiling is 10-2 if Philo or Jones Jr. takes a leap and McClain stays healthy all season.
takeaways: 2026 portal class addressed QB, WR, TE, S, RB, and OL — McClain is a prior-cycle enrollee
Defense is ahead of schedule; offense needs red-zone consistency
QB battle is genuinely dead even — no lean as of early June
Year 1 projection: 8-4, with 10-2 ceiling if OL and QB click

title: Aaron Philo Film Study: Who Is The New UF Quarterback?
author: GatorVault Film Desk
excerpt: Georgia Tech transfer Aaron Philo brings pro-style mechanics and anticipatory throwing to Gainesville. We broke down 12 games of film to map his strengths, weaknesses, and fit in {{ coach.OC }}'s offense.
body:
Aaron Philo arrived from Georgia Tech as a 6'3", 215-pound quarterback with 47% third-down conversion rate — the best on his GT team in 2025. The film confirms what the stats suggest: Philo is a rhythm passer who thrives when the offense stays on schedule.

Strengths: Philo's best trait is anticipatory throwing. On mesh concepts and levels routes, he throws before the receiver breaks — a skill that translates directly to {{ coach.OC }}'s RPO-heavy system. His arm strength is above average; he drives intermediate crossers with velocity and places back-shoulder throws on the boundary. Mobility is underrated — he's not a runner, but he extends plays with subtle pocket movement.

Weaknesses: Philo can be late on blitz recognition. Against simulated pressure in spring, he held the ball a beat too long on three occasions that would have been sacks in live action. His deep ball accuracy is streaky — when his base is clean, he drops it in; under pressure, he tends to overthrow.

Scheme fit: {{ coach.OC }}'s offense asks the QB to read conflict defenders on RPOs and make quick decisions on play-action. Philo's GT tape shows elite processing on these concepts. The question is whether UF's rebuilt OL can give him a clean enough pocket to operate. If they can, Philo projects as a top-30 QB nationally by Week 8.

Competition note: Tramell Jones Jr. offers more dynamic rushing ability and special teams experience. Philo wins if the offense needs a distributor who limits turnovers. Jones Jr. wins if {{ coach.HC }} wants a dual-threat who can extend drives with his legs.
takeaways: Best trait: anticipatory throwing on mesh and levels concepts
Main concern: blitz recognition and deep-ball consistency under pressure
Fits {{ coach.OC }}'s RPO system if the rebuilt OL protects him
Projects top-30 nationally by Week 8 in best-case scenario

title: War Room: 2026 Schedule Win Probability Model
author: GatorVault Analytics
excerpt: Our proprietary model simulates every game 10,000 times using roster data, returning production, and opponent strength. Here's where UF wins, where it's vulnerable, and the two games that decide the season.
body:
GatorVault's 2026 win probability model factors in returning production (52nd nationally), portal additions (12th nationally), strength of schedule (4th in SEC), and coaching stability. We ran 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations for each game.

Lock games (90%+): FAU (94%), Samford (97%), USF (91%), Kentucky (88%). These are games UF should win regardless of QB — the talent gap is too large.

Toss-ups (40-60%): Auburn away (52%), LSU home (58%), Tennessee away (47%), FSU away (48%). These four games determine whether UF finishes 8-4 or 10-2. Auburn is the first true test — Jordan-Hare in September with a new QB is a recipe for variance.

Stretch games (under 45%): Georgia (41%), Texas away (38%), Missouri home (55% — closer than you think). Georgia in Jacksonville is the hardest game on the schedule. Our model gives UF a puncher's chance only if Baugh goes for 100+ yards and Woods generates consistent pressure.

Expected wins: 8.3. Most likely record: 8-4 (34% probability), followed by 9-3 (22%) and 7-5 (18%). The model's biggest swing factor is QB play — a full season from a settled starter adds 1.2 expected wins compared to a rotating duo.

Bowl projection: 78% chance of a bowl game. Citrus Bowl (Orlando) is the most likely destination at 8-4. A 10-2 finish opens the door to New Year's Six conversation.
takeaways: Model projects 8.3 expected wins — most likely record is 8-4
Four toss-up games: Auburn, LSU, Tennessee, FSU decide the season
Georgia (41%) and Texas (38%) are the hardest matchups
Settled QB starter adds 1.2 expected wins vs. a rotating duo

title: Summer Camp Preview: 5 Position Battles to Watch
author: GatorVault Staff
excerpt: Fall camp opens August 2nd. These five position battles will shape the starting lineup, the two-deep, and how {{ coach.HC }}'s first season unfolds.
body:
Summer workouts are closed to media, but our sources inside the program point to five battles that will dominate fall camp headlines.

1. Quarterback — Jones Jr. vs. Philo: The biggest story of the offseason. Both quarterbacks have had strong spring moments. Jones Jr. offers dual-threat ability and special teams experience. Philo brings pro-style poise and third-down efficiency. {{ coach.HC }} has said he won't name a starter until after the FAU game — expect a split in Weeks 1-3.

2. Right Guard — Lovett vs. Shanahan: A genuine dead heat. Both are R-Juniors with starting experience elsewhere. The winner likely starts; the loser becomes the top backup at RG and C.

3. STAR/Nickel — Clark with Coleman rotation: Kanye Clark is the projected starter at STAR (GatorCountry / Prep Prospect Report). DJ Coleman — not Dylan Purter — is the veteran who rotates in as the second nickel while also backing up at FS. Purter and Owens are development pieces behind them.

4. WR (X) — Wilson vs. Mays: Dallas Wilson is projected at X after spring practice reps, though he did not play in the spring game. Micah Mays brings size and veteran savvy and will push in fall camp.

5. OL Right Side — Jones (RT) vs. depth: Caden Jones holds RT, but Eagan Boyer and Javarii Luckas are pushing. If Jones struggles in pass protection against Auburn, Boyer could get a look.

Fall camp report cards drop weekly inside the Vault starting August 5th.
takeaways: QB battle likely splits Weeks 1-3 before a starter emerges
RG (Lovett vs. Shanahan) is a genuine dead heat; STAR is Clark with Coleman rotating in
Wilson projects at WR(X) but did not play in the spring game; Mays is pushing
Weekly fall camp report cards start August 5th in the Vault
